sections.name: Multiple Choice
sections.notes: Most questions are stimulus-based (data tables, text passages, charts, maps, political cartoons). Skill categories: Concept Application ~35%, Country Comparison ~25%, Data Analysis ~20%, Source Analysis ~10%, Argumentation ~10%.

sections.name: Free Response
frq_types: Conceptual Analysis (1 question) — define and apply a political science concept using course country examples
Quantitative Analysis (1 question) — read and interpret a data set, chart, or table comparing course countries
Comparative Analysis (1 question) — compare two or more course countries on a specified dimension
Argument Essay (1 question) — construct a defensible claim using evidence from course countries

six_course_countries.key_facts: Parliamentary system; unitary state with devolution to Scotland, Wales, Northern Ireland; rule of law; FPTP elections; strong civil society; Brexit 2020
Presidential system; federal state; democratic transition from PRI one-party rule (2000 Fox victory); INE independent election commission; gender quotas; mixed electoral system
Presidential system; federal state; returned to civilian rule 1999; 250+ ethnic groups; 25% geographic threshold rule; PDP/APC two-dominant-party alternation; resource-rich rentier state

Semi-presidential system; competitive authoritarian regime; six tools of managed dominance (registration, thresholds, courts, media, party rules, electoral rules); state media nationalization; rentier state
Theocracy; unitary state; Supreme Leader above elected president; Guardian Council vets candidates; velayat-e faqih doctrine; no formal political parties; Green Movement 2009

One-party state (CPC); unitary state; Politburo Standing Committee holds real power; NPC is rubber-stamp legislature; rule by law not rule of law; Great Firewall; SEZs; rentier-adjacent resource control

tips: Every answer about Comp Gov must be grounded in at least one of the six course countries — abstract definitions alone are never enough
Know the regime category for each country: UK/Mexico/Nigeria are democratic; Russia is hybrid; China/Iran are authoritarian — this classification drives many MCQ and FRQ questions
The Conceptual Analysis FRQ always asks you to define a concept AND apply it to a specific course country — practice both parts
The Comparative Analysis FRQ requires identifying both a similarity AND a difference between countries — never answer with only one dimension
Quantitative Analysis FRQ: describe the data pattern first, then explain it using a course concept — never skip the explanation step
Federal states in this course: Mexico, Nigeria, Russia. Unitary states: China, Iran, UK. Students frequently misclassify Russia as unitary — it is federal.
Iran's electoral institutions (president, Majles) are elected, but the Guardian Council vets all candidates — always note this constraint when discussing Iranian participation
Russia is classified as a hybrid / competitive authoritarian regime, not a full democracy or a full authoritarian state — this nuance appears in almost every Russia question

units.name: Political Systems, Regimes, and Governments
key_topics: Regime vs. government vs. state vs. nation distinctions (PAU-1.A)
Democracy vs. authoritarianism spectrum: rule of law indicators, media freedom, electoral fairness (PAU-1.B)
Authoritarian regime subtypes: hybrid/illiberal (Russia), one-party state (China), theocracy (Iran), military regime (Nigeria historical) (PAU-1.B.3)
Democratization goals and democratic consolidation; backsliding (PAU-1.C)
Gender quotas and proportional representation as democratization tools — Mexico primary example (PAU-1.C.2)
Sources of power and authority: constitutions, religion, military, political parties (PAU-1.D.1)

CPC military control (China); velayat-e faqih / 1979 Revolution (Iran); PRI decline + 2000 transition (Mexico); managed democracy (Russia); devolution (UK); military-to-multiparty (Nigeria) (PAU-1.D.1a-e)
Federal vs. unitary systems: Mexico/Nigeria/Russia are federal; China/Iran/UK are unitary (PAU-2.A.1)
UK devolution to Scotland, Wales, Northern Ireland as unitary-state flexibility (PAU-1.D.1e)
Sources and sustainability of legitimacy: elections, nationalism, economic growth, ideology, religion, tradition (LEG-1.A-1.B)
Internal actors threatening or reinforcing stability: MEND/MOSOP (Nigeria), Zapatistas (Mexico), Green Movement (Iran), Chechen separatists (Russia) (LEG-1.C.1)
Political science methods: quantitative vs. qualitative; correlation vs. causation; empirical vs. normative (MPA-1.A)
Data indices: HDI, GDP per capita, Gini index, Freedom House, Transparency International, Failed States Index (MPA-1.A.8)
learning_objectives: PAU-1.A: Describe differences between regimes, states, nations, and governments
PAU-1.B: Describe democracy and authoritarianism
PAU-1.C: Explain the process and goals of democratization
PAU-1.D: Explain sources of power and authority in political systems
PAU-2.A: Describe federal and unitary systems among course countries
LEG-1.A: Describe the sources of political legitimacy for different regime types
LEG-1.B: Explain how governments maintain legitimacy
LEG-1.C: Explain how internal actors influence state authority and stability
MPA-1.A: Explain how political scientists construct knowledge about political systems

country_primary_examples.United Kingdom: Rule of law anchor; unitary state with devolution; democratic consolidation; traditional authority (monarchy); civil society strength
country_primary_examples.Russia: Managed democracy / illiberal democracy; recentralization; 2011 election protests; state-controlled media as legitimacy indicator
country_primary_examples.China: One-party state; rule by law not rule of law; CPC-military link; Great Firewall as intro; economic performance legitimacy
country_primary_examples.Iran: 1979 Revolution as regime change paradigm; velayat-e faqih as source of authority; theocracy; Green Movement 2009
country_primary_examples.Mexico: Democratization from PRI one-party rule; 2000 Fox election as democratic milestone; INE creation; gender quotas; federal system
country_primary_examples.Nigeria: Military-to-civilian transition (1999); federal system; MEND/MOSOP as internal actors; Boko Haram; ethnic cleavage complexity

high_frequency_exam_concepts: Regime vs. government distinction — the most tested conceptual pair in Unit 1
Federal vs. unitary: Mexico/Nigeria/Russia (federal) vs. China/Iran/UK (unitary) — often a direct table question
Sources of legitimacy: democratic (elections) vs. authoritarian (nationalism, economic growth, ideology, religion)
Democratization goals (PAU-1.C.1a-g) and how they can be blocked (corruption, election rules, civil liberty restrictions)
Rule of law (democratic) vs. rule by law (China) — definitional pair tested frequently
1979 Iranian Revolution as the clearest course example of sudden regime change via revolution
Mexico 2000 election (Fox/PAN) as the clearest course example of incremental-democratic government change
Devolution in the UK: the CED's example of how a unitary state can decentralize without becoming federal
common_question_types: Data table MCQ comparing Freedom House, HDI, or Gini scores across the six countries — test correlation vs. causation
Text passage stimulus describing a regime's authority source — classify as charismatic, traditional, or rational-legal
Comparative MCQ: Which pair of countries are both federal systems? / Which uses rule by law?
Conceptual Analysis FRQ applying democratization goals or federal vs. unitary distinction to a named country
Argument Essay using internal actors (Zapatistas, MEND/MOSOP, Green Movement) as evidence for stability claims
exam_tips: Distinguish regime (durable rules for holding power) from government (specific officeholders at a given moment) — a regime can survive many governments
Russia's regime classification is 'hybrid' or 'competitive authoritarian' or 'managed democracy' — never classify Russia as simply 'authoritarian' or simply 'democratic'
The UK is a unitary state even though it has devolution — Westminster retains parliamentary sovereignty and could theoretically repeal the devolution statutes
Authoritarian regimes can have legitimacy (China via economic growth; Iran via Islamic ideology; Russia via nationalism) — legitimacy is not exclusive to democracies
Correlation does not prove causation: that richer countries tend to be more democratic does not mean wealth causes democracy — name confounding variables in FRQ arguments

units.name: Political Institutions
key_topics: Parliamentary system (UK): legislature selects and removes PM; fused powers; vote of no confidence (PAU-3.A.1)
Presidential system (Mexico, Nigeria): separately elected executive and legislature; fixed terms; cabinet responsible to executive (PAU-3.A.2)
Semi-presidential system (Russia): popularly elected president + PM accountable to legislature (PAU-3.A.3)

Executive structures by country: UK (PM + monarch as head of state); Russia (president + prime minister dual executive); China (president + premier + General Secretary + CMC chair consolidated); Iran (Supreme Leader above elected president); Mexico (single-term president as head of state and government); Nigeria (president as head of state and government) (PAU-3.C.2)
Iran's Supreme Leader: commander in chief, appoints judiciary head, appoints Expediency Council and half of Guardian Council (PAU-3.C.2.b)
Executive term limits: advantages (check on power, fresh leadership) and disadvantages (lame duck, policy discontinuity) — Mexico's single 6-year term is the key example (PAU-3.C.3)
Removal of executives: UK vote of no confidence; Nigeria impeachment by two-thirds; Iran's Majles impeaches president; Assembly of Experts removes Supreme Leader (PAU-3.D.1)
Unicameral legislatures: China's NPC (rubber-stamp, legitimizing function) and Iran's Majles (constrained by Guardian Council) (PAU-3.E.1.a-b)

Bicameral legislatures: Mexico (Chamber of Deputies + Senate); Nigeria (National Assembly: Senate + House of Representatives); Russia (State Duma + Federation Council appointed); UK (House of Commons + House of Lords appointed) (PAU-3.E.1.c-f)
China's Politburo Standing Committee: the real power center constraining the NPC (PAU-3.F.1.a)
Iran's Guardian Council (vets candidates + reviews legislation) and Expediency Council (resolves disputes) (PAU-3.F.1.c-d)

Judicial systems: UK (common law, rule of law, Supreme Court); China (rule by law, CPC controls judiciary); Iran (Sharia-based, Supreme Leader appoints head); Mexico/Nigeria (judicial review in transition); Russia (judicial review exists but used against opposition) (PAU-3.G.1)
Judicial independence factors: appointment process, tenure, removal, professional background (PAU-3.H.1)
learning_objectives: PAU-3.A: Describe parliamentary, presidential, and semi-presidential systems
PAU-3.B: Compare institutional relations among system types
PAU-3.C: Explain the structure, function, and change of executive leadership
PAU-3.D: Describe procedures for removal of executive leadership
PAU-3.E: Describe legislative structures and functions
PAU-3.F: Explain how legislative powers are constrained by other institutions
PAU-3.G: Describe the structure and functions of judiciaries
PAU-3.H: Explain the importance of independent judiciaries

country_primary_examples.United Kingdom: Parliamentary system; vote of no confidence; House of Lords (appointed); common law; rule of law in judiciary; devolution disputes handled by Supreme Court
country_primary_examples.Russia: Semi-presidential system; Federation Council (appointed, not elected); State Duma; judicial system used against opposition
country_primary_examples.China: NPC as rubber-stamp / unicameral; Politburo Standing Committee holds real power; rule by law; CPC controls judicial appointments
country_primary_examples.Iran: Supreme Leader above elected president — the clearest dual authority example; Guardian Council (legislative constraint + candidate vetting); Expediency Council; Sharia-based judiciary
country_primary_examples.Mexico: Presidential system; single six-year term limit; bicameral Congress; judicial review in transition; Senate confirms Supreme Court nominees
country_primary_examples.Nigeria: Presidential system; bicameral National Assembly; impeachment by two-thirds; Sharia courts in northern states under federalism

high_frequency_exam_concepts: Parliamentary vs. presidential vs. semi-presidential: UK is parliamentary; Mexico/Nigeria are presidential; Russia is semi-presidential
China NPC vs. Politburo Standing Committee: formal vs. real power — the clearest authoritarian legislature example
Iran's Supreme Leader vs. elected president: unelected authority outranks elected authority
Guardian Council: Iran's institutional constraint on both the legislature and elections
Vote of no confidence (UK) vs. impeachment (Nigeria) — the two main executive removal mechanisms
Rule of law (UK, transitional Mexico/Nigeria) vs. rule by law (China) in judicial context
Executive term limits: advantages AND disadvantages — the CED lists both; Mexico's single-term limit is the primary example
Unicameral (China, Iran) vs. bicameral (Mexico, Nigeria, Russia, UK) — frequently appears as a direct country identification question
common_question_types: Short text passage describing an executive action — identify system type or explain which country it describes
Comparative table of legislative features (unicameral/bicameral, elected/appointed upper house) — identify countries
Scenario about a parliamentary crisis — determine which removal mechanism applies
MCQ about China's NPC: why does it serve a 'legitimizing function' despite lacking real power?
MCQ distinguishing Iran's Guardian Council from the Expediency Council
Comparative Analysis FRQ comparing executive removal mechanisms in two course countries
exam_tips: Russia has a prime minister but is NOT parliamentary — Russia is semi-presidential because the president is separately elected and far more powerful than the PM
The UK has both a head of state (the monarch) and a head of government (the PM) — they are different people with different roles
China's NPC is constitutionally the most powerful institution, but in practice it rubber-stamps Politburo Standing Committee decisions — always note this formal vs. real power gap
Iran's president is elected by popular vote, but the Supreme Leader is above the president — elections in Iran do not give citizens control over the most powerful office
Independent judiciary does not just mean 'not corrupt' — it specifically means courts can rule against the executive without consequences, based on appointment security and tenure

units.name: Political Culture and Participation
key_topics: Civil society: voluntary associations (NGOs, media, trade unions, religious groups) autonomous from the state; strength varies by regime type (IEF-1.A)
Civil society as agent of democratization; role in exposing malfeasance and providing organizational experience (IEF-1.B)
Civil society registration laws in authoritarian regimes (Russia, China, Iran) restrict and monitor groups (IEF-1.A.2)
Political culture: collective attitudes about the role of government; shaped by geography, religion, and history (IEF-1.C.1-1.C.2)
Post-Soviet political culture (Russia): tolerance of strong-man leadership, low institutional trust; Islamic political culture (Iran): clerical rule as legitimate governance (IEF-1.C.2)
Political socialization: lifelong process; agents include family, school, peers, media, government; authoritarian regimes direct it more aggressively (IEF-1.C.3-1.C.5)
Six political ideologies: individualism, neoliberalism, communism (China), socialism, fascism, populism (IEF-1.C.6a-f)
Rule of law (democratic regimes, especially UK) vs. rule by law (authoritarian regimes, especially China) (IEF-1.D.1)
Post-materialism: shift from material to self-expression/quality-of-life values as societies develop; UK primary example (IEF-1.D.3)
Political participation: voluntary vs. coerced; formal (voting) vs. informal (protest, social media) (DEM-1.A.1-1.A.2)
Elections in authoritarian regimes: safety valve / illusion of influence — not genuine citizen control (DEM-1.A.4)
Referenda in the UK: Brexit (2016), devolution referenda, Scottish independence (2014) — the only course country with CED-named referenda (DEM-1.A.5)
Competitive authoritarian regime / illiberal democracy: Russia's classification — holds elections, restricts civil liberties (DEM-1.C.5)
Civil liberties restrictions: Great Firewall (China), media license revocation (Iran), state-owned broadcast media (Russia) (DEM-1.C.3)

Social cleavages by country: China (Han majority vs. Uighurs/Tibetans; regional development gap); Iran (Shi'a-Sunni; Persian-Kurdish-Azerbaijani); Mexico (Amerindian vs. mestizo; north-south regional); Nigeria (Hausa-Fulani/Yoruba/Igbo; Muslim north/Christian south — overlapping ethnic + religious + regional); Russia (ethnic Russian majority vs. Chechens); UK (English/Scottish/Welsh/Irish; Protestant-Catholic in Northern Ireland) (LEG-2.A.1)
State responses to cleavages: repression (China, Russia, Iran) vs. recognition/devolution (UK) vs. negotiation (Mexico Zapatistas) (LEG-2.B.2)
Separatist movements: China (Tibet/Xinjiang), Nigeria (historical Biafra), Russia (Chechnya), UK (Scotland); autonomy demands (not secession): Mexico (Zapatistas), UK (devolution) (LEG-2.B.4)
learning_objectives: IEF-1.A: Describe civil society
IEF-1.B: Explain the role of civil society among course countries
IEF-1.C: Explain how political culture relates to citizen behavior and the role of the state
IEF-1.D: Explain how political values and beliefs frame policy choices
DEM-1.A: Explain the nature and role of political participation
DEM-1.B: Explain how political participation affects and is affected by regime type
DEM-1.C: Explain the extent to which civil rights and liberties are protected or restricted
LEG-2.A: Describe politically relevant social cleavages
LEG-2.B: Explain how political and social cleavages affect citizen relationships and political stability

country_primary_examples.United Kingdom: Strong civil society; post-materialism; referenda (Brexit, devolution, Scottish independence); rule of law; UK's overlapping ethnic/regional cleavages (Scotland, Northern Ireland)
country_primary_examples.Russia: Competitive authoritarian / illiberal democracy; state media nationalization; restricted civil society; post-Soviet political culture; ethnic Russian vs. Chechen cleavage
country_primary_examples.China: Great Firewall; state-directed socialization; communism as ideology; Han/Uighur/Tibetan ethnic cleavage; civil society restricted through registration
country_primary_examples.Iran: Islamic political culture; media license revocation; Green Movement 2009 as suppressed oppositional participation; Shi'a-Sunni and Persian-Kurdish cleavages
country_primary_examples.Mexico: Neoliberalism and populism; Amerindian/mestizo ethnic cleavage; north-south regional divide; Zapatista movement as autonomy demand
country_primary_examples.Nigeria: Overlapping ethnic + religious + regional cleavages (the most complex cleavage profile); Hausa-Fulani/Yoruba/Igbo; Muslim north / Christian-animist south; Boko Haram as cleavage-driven terrorism

high_frequency_exam_concepts: Rule of law vs. rule by law — the single most tested distinction in Unit 3; UK exemplifies rule of law, China exemplifies rule by law
Civil society definition and how regime type restricts or enables it — UK (strong) vs. Russia/China/Iran (restricted)
Competitive authoritarian regime / Russia's DEM-1.C.5 classification — a named CED category students must apply precisely
Social cleavages by country — Nigeria's overlapping ethnic + religious + regional cleavages are tested most frequently
Political participation: voluntary vs. coerced; authoritarian elections as safety valve / illusion of influence
Political socialization: state-directed (China, Russia, Iran) vs. pluralist (UK); agents of socialization
Great Firewall (China), state media nationalization (Russia), media license revocation (Iran) — three named CED examples of civil liberties restrictions
UK referenda: Brexit, devolution, Scottish independence — the CED's only course-country referendum examples
common_question_types: Text passage comparing civil society conditions in two countries — identify regime type and explain restriction
Data table of press freedom index scores — draw conclusions about regime type and civil liberties
Map of ethnic/religious distribution (Nigeria's north-south divide; China's minority regions) — describe cleavage and its implications
Comparative table of cleavage types across countries — identify which country has which type
MCQ on authoritarian elections as safety valve — 'Which of the following best explains why authoritarian regimes hold elections?'
Comparative Analysis FRQ comparing state responses to cleavages (e.g., UK devolution vs. China repression)
exam_tips: Civil society is defined by its autonomy FROM the state — organizations that the state controls are not civil society

Authoritarian regimes hold elections regularly — high voter turnout in authoritarian states does not indicate democratic participation; it may indicate coerced participation or an illusion of influence
Post-materialism applies most clearly to the UK — it is the CED's only explicit post-materialist course country; do not apply it broadly to authoritarian regimes
Nigeria's cleavages are uniquely overlapping: ethnic AND religious AND regional divisions reinforce each other — this is why Nigeria appears most frequently in cleavage questions
The Green Movement (Iran, 2009) illustrates oppositional participation suppressed by an authoritarian regime — it is the CED's primary named example of mass protest under authoritarianism

units.name: Party and Electoral Systems and Citizen Organizations
key_topics: Electoral systems by country: UK (SMD/FPTP for House of Commons); Russia (mixed: half SMD + half PR with threshold for State Duma); Mexico (mixed: 300 SMD + 200 PR for Chamber of Deputies; 96 state + 32 PR for Senate; gender quotas); Nigeria (SMD for House of Representatives; 3 senators per state); Iran (direct election in SMD/multimember districts; candidates vetted by Guardian Council; reserved seats for non-Muslim minorities); China (indirect layered elections for NPC) (DEM-2.A.1)
FPTP/SMD effects: tends toward two-party dominance, strong constituency accountability, geographic representation, high disproportionality (DEM-2.B.2)
PR effects: tends toward multiparty systems, increases minority and women's representation (DEM-2.B.1, DEM-2.A.2)
Presidential election rules: Mexico uses plurality (most votes); Iran and Russia require absolute majority with runoff; Nigeria requires most votes AND 25% in two-thirds of states (DEM-2.B.3)
Nigeria's 25% geographic threshold: reflects federal character; winner needs national mandate across regions (DEM-2.B.3.b)
Guardian Council as ballot access restriction: vets and excludes reform-minded or un-Islamic candidates in Iran (DEM-2.B.4.a)
INE (Mexico) and INEC (Nigeria) as independent election management bodies created during democratic transitions (DEM-2.B.4.b)

Appointed upper chambers: UK House of Lords (PM recommendation + independent commission); Russia Federation Council (appointed by regional governors) — both serve different regime purposes (DEM-2.B.5)

Party systems: one-party (China: CPC + 8 minor consultative parties); dominant/managed (Russia: United Russia + 6 managed tools); multiparty transitioning from dominant (Mexico: PRI/PAN/PRD competition; corporatist to pluralist shift); multiparty with ethnic quotas (Nigeria: PDP/APC alternation); no formal parties (Iran: loosely formed alliances/factions); two-party tendency with regional parties (UK: Conservative/Labour + SNP, Plaid Cymru) (PAU-4.A)

Russia's 6 managed democracy tools: party registration requirements, legally registered parties only, court disqualifications, media access restrictions, threshold rules, elimination of gubernatorial elections (PAU-4.A.3)
Mexico's democratic transition mechanisms: eliminating el dedazo, privatizing SOEs (reduces patronage), subnational decentralization, INE/IFE creation (PAU-4.A.4)
El dedazo (Mexico): PRI president's informal power to hand-pick the next candidate; abolished as a key democratization reform (PAU-4.A.4)
Pluralism (autonomous competing interest groups; UK primary; Mexico transitioning) vs. corporatism (state-sanctioned single peak associations; Mexico historically; IEF-2.B.2)
Social movements vs. interest groups: social movements are broad and decentralized; interest groups are formally organized for specific policy goals (IEF-2.A.1-2.A.2)

Named social movements: Green Movement Iran 2009 (election fraud protest; suppressed); Zapatistas/EZLN Mexico 1994 (NAFTA + indigenous inequality); MEND (Niger Delta armed militant group); MOSOP (Ken Saro-Wiwa; Ogoni people non-violent advocacy); Boko Haram Nigeria (Islamist insurgency) (IEF-2.A.3)
Party discipline: strongest in UK's House of Commons; influences policy-making in parliamentary systems (PAU-4.A.7)
learning_objectives: DEM-2.A: Describe electoral systems and election rules among course countries
DEM-2.B: Explain how election rules serve different regime objectives
PAU-4.A: Describe characteristics of political party systems and party membership
PAU-4.B: Explain how party systems link citizen participation to policy making
IEF-2.A: Explain how social movements and interest groups affect social and political change
IEF-2.B: Describe pluralist and corporatist interest group systems

country_primary_examples.United Kingdom: FPTP/SMD → two-party tendency with regional exceptions (SNP, Plaid Cymru); appointed House of Lords; strong party discipline; pluralist interest group system
country_primary_examples.Russia: Managed democracy tools (6 mechanisms); mixed Duma electoral system; United Russia dominance; Federation Council appointed by regional governors; competitive authoritarian
country_primary_examples.China: One-party system (CPC + 8 minor consultative parties); indirect NPC elections; no genuine electoral competition
country_primary_examples.Iran: Guardian Council as ballot access restriction; no formal political parties (loosely formed alliances); reserved Majles seats for non-Muslim minorities; absolute majority + runoff for president
country_primary_examples.Mexico: Democratic transition from corporatism to pluralism; el dedazo abolition; INE creation; gender quotas in party lists; mixed electoral system; PAN/PRD/PRI multiparty competition; Zapatistas as named social movement
country_primary_examples.Nigeria: 25% geographic threshold for president; ethnic quotas; PDP/APC two-dominant-party alternation; INEC; MEND/MOSOP/Boko Haram as named social movements

high_frequency_exam_concepts: Comparing SMD/FPTP vs. PR effects on party systems — UK (FPTP → two-party tendency) vs. Mexico (mixed PR → multiparty)
Iran's Guardian Council as ballot access restriction — its function in limiting electoral competition
Mexico's democratic transition: INE/IFE, el dedazo abolition, PRI decline, corporatist-to-pluralist shift
Russia's 6 managed democracy tools (PAU-4.A.3) — each tool is individually testable
Nigeria's 25% geographic threshold: unique presidential election rule reflecting federal structure
Pluralism vs. corporatism: definitions + Mexico's transition as the named CED example
Green Movement (Iran 2009) as the key social movement example in an authoritarian state
Zapatistas (Mexico 1994) as the key social movement example combining indigenous rights and NAFTA grievances
MOSOP / Ken Saro-Wiwa as Nigeria's highest-yield person-based social movement example
common_question_types: Electoral rules comparison table: identify which country uses which system, what effect it has on party representation
Text passage about Iran's Guardian Council — what function does it serve? Compare to democratic EMBs
Scholar passage distinguishing pluralism from corporatism — identify which country matches which model
MCQ on Mexico's democratic transition — what specific change illustrates which aspect of democratization?
Comparative Analysis FRQ: compare the electoral rules of two countries and explain their different effects on party competition
Text passage describing a named social movement (Green Movement, Zapatistas, MOSOP) — classify as social movement or interest group and explain
exam_tips: China has NINE parties but only one has power — the eight minor parties serve consultative functions under CPC supervision and cannot challenge CPC governance
Iran does not have formal political parties — always use 'political factions' or 'loosely formed alliances' when describing Iranian political competition
Mexico's presidential election uses plurality (most votes wins), NOT a majority requirement — this is different from Iran and Russia which require absolute majority + potential runoff

Nigeria's 25% geographic threshold is unique among the six countries and appears frequently — a presidential winner must have national geographic distribution of support, not just the most total votes
The PRI is the historical Mexico example; PAN, PRD are the opposition parties named in the CED. Morena is a contemporary party but is not a primary CED example.

units.name: Political and Economic Changes and Development
key_topics: Economic globalization: increasing cross-national interconnection through trade, investment, capital flows; challenges for regime and cultural sovereignty (IEF-3.A.1)
Economic liberalization: reducing state economic role — cutting tariffs and subsidies, privatizing industries, opening to FDI (IEF-3.D.1)
Neoliberalism: ideology calling for free markets, privatization, deregulation; promoted by IMF/World Bank; mixed effects — growth + inequality (IEF-3.E.3)
Rentier states: Iran, Nigeria, Russia — derive primary revenue from oil/gas exports rather than citizen taxation; linked to weak accountability (LEG-5.A.1)

Resource curse: political and economic distortions from oil wealth — lack of diversification, corruption, revenue volatility, currency overvaluation, reduced accountability, absence of democracy (LEG-5.A.2)
Special economic zones (SEZs): China's coastal market enclaves that attract FDI while the broader economy remains state-controlled (IEF-3.B.1a)
Maquiladora zones: Mexico's export assembly factories near the US border; created by NAFTA/trade liberalization; drove south-to-north internal migration (IEF-3.B.1b, LEG-4.A.2c)
Privatization spectrum: UK allows most private resource control; China allows least private resource control (IEF-3.B.2)
Putin's re-nationalization of Russian oil/gas industries and foreign investment limitations (IEF-3.B.1d)
NNPC (Nigeria): state oil company collaborating with MNCs in joint ventures (IEF-3.B.1c)
Pemex privatization (Mexico): partial opening of oil sector to competition (IEF-3.B.1b)
IMF/World Bank structural adjustment programs (SAPs): conditions for financial assistance (privatization, tariff cuts, reduced subsidies) (LEG-3.A.1)
Import substitution industrialization (ISI): alternative strategy of raising tariffs and building domestic industry to reduce foreign dependency (LEG-3.A.2)
Supranational organizations with sovereign powers over members: ECOWAS (Nigeria), EU (UK — departed via Brexit 2020), WTO (LEG-3.A.3)
NAFTA/USMCA effects on Mexico: maquiladora expansion, internal migration (south to north), regional disparities, middle class growth, Zapatista response (IEF-3.B.1b, LEG-4.A.2c)
HDI as development measure combining GDP per capita, life expectancy, and education — preferred over GDP alone (IEF-3.E.1)
Gini index as inequality measure — high Gini = high inequality; Mexico and Nigeria among highest in course; UK relatively lower (IEF-3.E.1)
Brain drain: skilled emigration from Iran and Nigeria to escape repression, corruption, or limited opportunity (LEG-4.A.2b)
UK aging population and NHS pressure: positive net migration + rising elderly dependency strain public health system (LEG-4.A.4)
China rural-to-urban and west-to-east internal migration from SEZs and liberalization policies (LEG-4.A.2a)
China's one-child policy: demographic intervention reducing population growth; now reversed (LEG-4.A.5)
Social welfare policies as legitimacy tools: governments improve health care, education, poverty relief to maintain public support (LEG-3.B.2)
Gender equity policies: Mexico's gender quotas in elections; Iran's limited gender equity rules in Majles voting; Nigeria's north-south gender education gap (LEG-3.B.1)
Challenges to regime sovereignty from globalization: MNC conflicts, cultural backlash, economic sanctions on Iran/Russia (IEF-3.C.1)
Environmental consequences of industrialization: air pollution (China primary), urban sprawl, fossil fuel dependence (LEG-3.C.1)
learning_objectives: IEF-3.A: Explain how global economic and technological forces influence political policies and culture
IEF-3.B: Compare political responses to global market forces
IEF-3.C: Explain how globalization creates challenges to regime sovereignty
IEF-3.D: Describe economic and political liberalization policies
IEF-3.E: Explain the adoption of and consequences associated with economic liberalization policies
LEG-3.A: Explain how international and supranational organizations influence domestic policymakers
LEG-3.B: Explain how governments adapt social policies to address change
LEG-3.C: Explain how rapid industrialization has produced radical changes in governmental policies
LEG-4.A: Explain political causes and consequences of demographic changes
LEG-5.A: Explain how natural resources affect political and economic development

country_primary_examples.United Kingdom: Most private resource control; positive net migration and social tensions; aging population / NHS pressure; Brexit as EU supranational departure; cultural backlash to globalization (Brexit nationalism)
country_primary_examples.Russia: Rentier state; Putin's re-nationalization of oil/gas; revenue volatility; cultural backlash to Western influence; economic sanctions; managed democracy linked to resource control
country_primary_examples.China: SEZs as controlled liberalization enclaves; least private resource control; rural-to-urban and west-to-east migration; one-child policy; environmental policy responses to industrialization; Gini inequality growth
country_primary_examples.Iran: Rentier state; brain drain; resource curse; economic sanctions constraining sovereignty; limited gender equity policies; revenue volatility from oil
country_primary_examples.Mexico: NAFTA/USMCA effects — maquiladora zones, south-to-north migration, regional disparities; Pemex partial privatization; ISI history; gender quotas; middle class growth from liberalization
country_primary_examples.Nigeria: Rentier state (NNPC/MNC joint ventures); resource curse in Niger Delta; brain drain; 25% geographic threshold linking to federal demographic structure; ECOWAS membership; gender education gap

high_frequency_exam_concepts: Rentier state definition + Iran/Nigeria/Russia as the three named examples — the most tested Unit 5 concept
Resource curse political outcomes (LEG-5.A.2a-i) — each sub-outcome is individually testable; weak accountability is the most distinctive
Economic liberalization definition (IEF-3.D.1): reducing tariffs/subsidies, privatizing industries, opening to FDI
SEZs in China: market-oriented enclaves while broader economy remains state-controlled
SAPs (IMF conditionality): countries receiving loans must privatize, cut tariffs, reduce subsidies
NAFTA effects on Mexico: maquiladoras, internal migration, regional disparities, but also middle class growth — mixed effects
HDI vs. GDP per capita as development measures — HDI is more comprehensive; the CED uses it for six-country comparison
UK as most private / China as least private on the resource control spectrum (IEF-3.B.2)
Putin's re-nationalization: Russia reversed privatization of oil/gas under Putin — unique among course countries
Brain drain: Iran and Nigeria are the named CED examples — driven by political repression as much as economic hardship
common_question_types: HDI table comparing all six countries — describe patterns, draw comparative inferences about development vs. regime type
Gini coefficient bar chart — identify which countries have higher inequality and explain using neoliberalism or resource curse
Oil revenue as % of GDP line graph for Iran/Nigeria/Russia — test rentier state and resource curse concepts
Text passage on neoliberal policy effects — evaluate whether a claim about growth or inequality is supported
IMF conditionality document excerpt — identify this as a structural adjustment program; explain sovereignty implications
Quantitative Analysis FRQ combining HDI data with freedom house scores — test relationship between development and democratization (remember: correlation, not causation)
exam_tips: All three rentier states (Iran, Nigeria, Russia) share the same resource curse political outcomes — weak governmental accountability, corruption, lack of diversification, revenue volatility
SEZs in China do not mean China has a free market — they are geographically limited enclaves; China still has the least private resource control among the six countries
NAFTA had mixed effects on Mexico — it grew the middle class and maquiladora zones in the north, but also caused internal migration and regional disparities and hurt southern agricultural workers
Supranational organizations (EU, ECOWAS, WTO) have sovereign powers over member states in certain policy areas — this is different from purely advisory international organizations
Brain drain in Iran and Nigeria is driven by political repression and corruption, not only poverty — this is a frequently tested CED distinction (LEG-4.A.2b)
